2605.06506.title: The Frequency Confound in Language-Model Surprisal and Metaphor Novelty
2605.06506.paper_summary: Language-model (LM) surprisal is widely used as a proxy for contextual predictability and has been reported to correlate with metaphor novelty judgments. However, surprisal is tightly intertwined with lexical frequency. We explore this interaction on metaphor novelty ratings using two different word frequency measures. We analyse surprisal estimates from eight Pythia model sizes and 154 training checkpoints. Across settings, word frequency is a stronger predictor of metaphor novelty than surprisal. Across training stages, the surprisal--novelty association peaks at an early stage and then falls again, mirroring a similarly timed increase in the surprisal--frequency association. These results suggest that the often-reported optimal LM surprisal settings may incorrectly associate contextual predictability with metaphor novelty and processing difficulty, whereas lexical frequency may be the major underlying factor.
2605.06506.paper_summary_zh: 語言模型 (LM) 的驚訝度被廣泛用作上下文可預測性的代理，並且已被報導與隱喻新穎性評價相關。然而，驚訝度與詞彙頻率緊密相互交織。我們使用兩種不同的詞頻測量來探討這種交互作用對隱喻新穎性評分的影響。我們分析了來自八個 Pythia 模型大小和 154 個訓練檢查點的驚訝度估計。在各種設置中，詞頻對隱喻新穎性的預測能力強於驚訝度。在訓練階段中，驚訝度與新穎性的關聯在早期階段達到高峰，然後再次下降，這與驚訝度與頻率的關聯的增長時間相似。這些結果表明，經常報導的最佳 LM 驚訝度設置可能錯誤地將上下文可預測性與隱喻新穎性和處理難度相關聯，而詞彙頻率可能是主要的潛在因素。
2605.06506.author: Omar Momen et.al.
